title: Mohio
content:
Problem Statement

As a product professional, I’ve experienced the inefficiency of reworking the same core information across multiple formats and tools. In my roles as a business analyst, product owner, and now product manager, I’ve found myself constantly duplicating effort:

  • Writing detailed documents in Word that are hard to digest and validate.

  • Converting those into user stories or slide decks—reshaping the same content over and over.

  • Copying backlog items into roadmaps, and then again into presentations.

  • Watching testers rewrite stories into functional tests.

  • Turning test scenarios into release notes and help pages.

This cycle of copy-paste-driven documentation leads to a bloated, fragile process where changes must be manually synced across formats. Despite different outputs, the meaning rarely changes—just its representation does.

Assumption

Knowledge isn’t a static document, presentation, or whiteboard—it’s a system of reusable thoughts. Each thought exists only once, adapts to its audience in different ways, and connects to others in a dynamic, structured network.

Solution

Niklas Luhmann’s Zettelkasten (Slip-Box) is a personal knowledge management system based on atomic, interconnected notes. It offers a powerful conceptual model for transforming fragmented content into a modular, reusable knowledge network.

Here is an example of inline code in context.

‘This is a quote block highlighting an important point in the document.’

Here’s a link to Placehold.co for placeholder images.

Core Principles

  1. Atomic Blocks: Information is composed of atomic, uniquely identifiable blocks, each representing a single, clear idea.

  2. Multi-Format Adaptation: While the format of information may vary, the underlying blocks remain the same.

  3. Semantic Integrity: Blocks can be rephrased, shortened, or reformatted to match different audiences.

  4. Linking & Associations: Content exists in a network, not a tree.

  5. Bidirectional Connections: Every block maintains backlinks, creating a web of meaning.

  6. Emergent Structure: There’s no fixed hierarchy; structure emerges organically.

Underlined text and strikethrough text example.

Tip: Use atomic blocks and semantic linking to reduce duplicate effort and keep your knowledge network adaptable and bloated lean.
